coredata.dc:title: Solar-driven adaptive radiative cooling coating with polymer carbon dots-enhanced photoluminescence for urban skin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Quan
Chun Wong, Ho
Chen, Jianheng
Li, Pei
Lu, Lin
coredata.dc:description:
Adaptive passive daytime radiative cooling coating adjusts cooling capacity on an as-needed basis, making it an advanced zero-energy building cooling technology for urban skin. Currently, research mostly focuses on temperature-driven mechanisms, constrained by the limited availability of phase change materials. To address this challenge, this study pioneers the fabrication of a Solar-driven Adaptive Radiative Cooling (SARC) coating, which can adjust cooling capacity based on solar irradiance. To achieve this objective, the polymer Carbon Dots (CDs), PEI/Poly(St-co-BA)@CDs, are innovatively incorporated in SARC coating as the photoluminescent materials to convert the absorbed solar energy into light energy. Notably, the polymer-based CDs enable the design of Smart Cooling Beads (SCBs) whose surfaces are hydrophilic, making it easier to get dissolved in aqueous solutions. Moreover, the CDs were evenly distributed on the surfaces of SCBs, allowing it to achieve a higher quantum yield, recorded as 33.6 %. Illustrated with an aqueous coating example for demonstration, the effective solar reflectance of developed SARC coating increases from 92.5 % to 95 % under solar radiation. Furthermore, the adaptive cooling performance (1.5 °C to 3.2 °C lower than static coatings) varies with solar radiation (from 220 W. m−
2 to 880 W. m−2 ), demonstrating an energy efficiency improvement. These findings validate the SARC coating as a simple, feasible, environmentally friendly, and scalable solution for the advancement of next-generation smart urban skin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar-driven radiative cooling
Photoluminescence
Low-carbon buildings
Polymer carbon dots

The work described in this paper was substantially supported by the CRF (Project No. C5051-22GF), the NSFC/RGC Joint Research Scheme sponsored by the Research Grants Council of Hong Kong, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. N_PolyU513/18), The Hong Kong Polytechnic University through Projects of RISE (Project No. Q-CDAJ), and Research Institute for Sustainable Urban Development (Project No. 1-BBWX).

Q. Gong, and H.C.Wong, contributed equally to this work. The work described in this paper was substantially supported by a grant from the NSFC /RGC Joint Research Scheme sponsored by the Research Grants Council of Hong Kong, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. N_PolyU513/18), The Hong Kong Polytechnic University through Projects of RISE (Project No. Q-CDAJ ), and Research Institute for Sustainable Urban Development (Project No. 1-BBWX ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Concept of SARC coating; a) Schematic representation comparing of normal RC and SARC; b) Simulated cooling temperatures illustrating the comparison between the normal RC (red) and SARC (blue); c) Simulated effective spectrum of a SARC with varying Quantum Yield (QY); d) Comparison of adaptive reflectance for different RCs; e) Schematic of our developed SARC for urban skin. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Microstructures and optical property of Fluorescent nanoparticles (FPs); a) chemical structure of PEI750k/Poly(St-co-BA) fluorescent nanoparticles; b) SEM images depicting of FPs@CDs; c) TEM of FPs@CDs; d) Size distribution of FPs (PDI = 0.05 (±0.02) @ pH = 4.19, ζ-potential = +42.6 mV (±0.8) @ pH = 4.37); e) Photoluminescence spectrum of the PEI750k/Poly(St-co-BA) (1000 ppm @ pH = 5.8). f) Photoluminescence spectrum of the FPs@CDs (100 ppm @ pH = 6.5).

Characterization of the smart cooling beads and SARC coating; a) SEM images of uncoated hollow glass beads; b) SEM images of the coated SCBs; c) Optical picture and SEM images of the SCBs; d) Photoluminescence test comparison of SARC coating (left) and normal PDRC coating (right); e) SEM images of the SARC coating.

Enhanced solar reflectivity analysis of SARC coating; a) Comparison of Quantum Yields (360 nm); d) Quantum yields of FPs@CDs at different bands; c) Effective Solar Absorptivity of SARC coating: Blue line(Solid) is the measured Effective Solar Absorption (ESA), Red line(dotted) is the simulated ESA based on the QYs; d) Temperature changes of the SARC coating under the indoor solar simulator; e) Effective solar reflectance and fluorescence contribution; f) Enhanced cooling temperature of SARC coating at different solar irradiances versus normal PDRC coating without fluorescence. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cooling performance comparison of SARC coating and normal RC; a) Measured spectrum of the SARC coating and normal RC coating; b) Temperature measured for ambient air, and SARC coating and normal RC coating; c) Resource map of maximum temperature drop; d) Resource map of maximum cooling power; e), f) Temperature difference varies with solar radiation; g) Cooling energy saving as building roof.

head.ce:title: Solar-driven adaptive radiative cooling coating with polymer carbon dots-enhanced photoluminescence for urban skin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Building Environment and Energy Engineering, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Building Environment and Energy Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Building Environment and Energy Engineering, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Biology and Chemical Technology, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Biology and Chemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Biology and Chemical Technology, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Adaptive passive daytime radiative cooling coating adjusts cooling capacity on an as-needed basis, making it an advanced zero-energy building cooling technology for urban skin. Currently, research mostly focuses on temperature-driven mechanisms, constrained by the limited availability of phase change materials. To address this challenge, this study pioneers the fabrication of a Solar-driven Adaptive Radiative Cooling (SARC) coating, which can adjust cooling capacity based on solar irradiance. To achieve this objective, the polymer Carbon Dots (CDs), PEI/Poly(St-co-BA)@CDs, are innovatively incorporated in SARC coating as the photoluminescent materials to convert the absorbed solar energy into light energy. Notably, the polymer-based CDs enable the design of Smart Cooling Beads (SCBs) whose surfaces are hydrophilic, making it easier to get dissolved in aqueous solutions. Moreover, the CDs were evenly distributed on the surfaces of SCBs, allowing it to achieve a higher quantum yield, recorded as 33.6 %. Illustrated with an aqueous coating example for demonstration, the effective solar reflectance of developed SARC coating increases from 92.5 % to 95 % under solar radiation. Furthermore, the adaptive cooling performance (1.5 °C to 3.2 °C lower than static coatings) varies with solar radiation (from 220 W. m−
2 to 880 W. m−2 ), demonstrating an energy efficiency improvement. These findings validate the SARC coating as a simple, feasible, environmentally friendly, and scalable solution for the advancement of next-generation smart urban skin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Conventional building cooling technologies that rely on electricity consume significant amounts of energy, whereas passive daytime radiative cooling (PDRC) offers a promising alternative by exchanging heat with the Universe through the atmospheric transparency window (8–13 μm) [1–3]. While static radiative coolers are typically designed with high reflectivity to achieve sub-ambient cooling under intense solar radiation [4–11], adaptive PDRC coatings that can dynamically modulate cooling performance in response to environmental changes are more suitable for integration into smart urban skins [12–14]

However, most adaptive PDRC focuses on temperature-responsive radiative cooling coatings, constrained by the limited availability of phase change materials like VO2 and paraffin [15–18]. Beyond temperature, other the solar radiation aslo plays crucial roles in radiative cooling. Hence, photoluminescent materials, capable of converting absorbed solar energy into light emission, have the potential to form the basis of Solar-driven Adaptive Radiative Cooling (SARC) coatings. For example, the addition of fluorescent pigments (SrAl2O4:Eu2+, Dy3+, Yb3+) leads to an enhanced effective solar reflectance under the stronger solar radiation [19–21]. Besides that, the SARC with photoluminescent material can simultaneously possess colour modulation and high cooling performance. Because in a typical downshifting process of photoluminescence, upon excitation with a high-energy photon, nonradiative relaxation takes place followed by radiative relaxation, thereby resulting in the emission of a lower-energy photon. Therefore, in the fabrication of colourful coating, the perovskite nanocrystals with high Quantum Yield (QY) have attracted attention [22–28]. By controlling the ratio of CsPbX3 (X = Br, I), fabrics with a wide color gamut and chroma have been successfully designed and fabricated.

Despite some advancements in the field, the existing photoluminescent materials mainly rely on rare-earth based metals and perovskite materials, which pose challenges such as environmental pollution, coating dispersion instability and difficult modification. As a result, their use in radiative cooling applications, especially in areas with strict healthcare regulations like roof coatings, wearable devices, textiles, and more, is hindered. Therefore, there is an urgent demand to develop a new polymer-based luminescent material and seamlessly integrate it into the SARC using a scalable strategy. Carbon Dots (CDs) have garnered considerable attention as promising fluorescent nanoparticles due to their facile synthesis, robust fluorescence, exceptional photostability, remarkable biocompatibility, and minimal toxicity [29–31]. Typically, CDs possess sizes below 10 nm and exhibit surface functionality [32]. However, the utilization of CDs in radiative cooling coatings remains relatively unexplored, with only one reported study to show the outstanding UV-blocking capacity [33].

This study pioneers the development of a scalable Solar-driven Adaptive Radiative Cooling (SARC) coating by using polymer Carbon Dots (CDs). Notably, in comparison to other metal-based photoluminescent materials, polymer carbon dots exhibit good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and easy surface functionalization. The facile modification of polymers allowed for the successful design of Smart Cooling Beads (SCBs), created by coating hollow glass beads with fluorescent polymer particles, PEI/Poly(St-co-BA), containing carbon dots. This novel SCBs design marks the first introduction of polymer carbon dots into a SARC coating, offering two main advantages. Firstly, SCBs with hydrophilic groups on the surface enhance compatibility with water, enabling the fabrication of uniform aqueous SARC roof coatings using aqueous solution-based methods. Secondly, the results reveal that polymer CDs are uniformly dispersed on the surface of SCBs, leading to SARC coatings with a high quantum yield of 33.6 %. This crosslinked strategy suggests the QY of polymer CDs have the potential to be further improved. Thus, SCBs serve as a useful and scalable versatile matrix of CDs in the SARC, opening up possibilities for large-scale applications in buildings. By examining the effects of photoluminescence spectrum and QYs, we have reconstructed the experimental spectrum of the developed coating, demonstrating its reduced solar-to-thermal capacity. The influence of fluorescence contribution, quantified at 2.5 % for adaptive solar reflectance, has been evaluated through calorimetric calculations. Thus, the effective solar reflectance of developed SARC coating is increased from 92.5 % to 95 % during the daytime, compared to the static normal RC. Additionally, the enhanced cooling effect of SCBs under solar radiation, ranging from 1.5 °C to 3.2 °C, varies in response to solar intensity (220 W. m−
2 to 880 W. m−2 ), showcasing its adaptive properties. Consequently, we observed a temperature reduction of approximately 20 °C under sunlight for continuous 72 h compared to a conventional concrete roof, indicating that the introduction of SCBs in the SARC roof coating maintains excellent radiative cooling performance. Moreover, during daytime hours (from 8:30 a.m. to 5:30p.m.) and under sunlight, the temperature of the SARC coating consistently remained lower than that of the normal RC without SCBs. Furthermore, cooling maps based on climate patterns have been generated to visually depict the significant enhancements in cooling performance and energy savings achieved by the SARC coating. Hence, this work introduces an innovative strategy for integrating scalable SARC roof coatings with metal-free photoluminescent materials, polymer carbon dots, opening up possibilities for the development of simple, feasible, environmental-friendly and scalable smart urban skins.

SARC coating with the photoluminescence can convert some absorbed photons into the light emission, which result in the higher cooling power compared to the normal Radiative Cooler (RC) without photoluminescence, as depicted in Fig. 1
a. From a perspective of the energy balance, the net cooling power (P net) is derived from the following equation:
(1)
P
net
T
=
P
Rad
T
-
P
atm
T
amb
-
P
sun
-
P
nonrad
PRad is the radiative cooling power from the surface, Patm is the downwelling atmospheric thermal radiation, and Pnonrad is the non-radiative power. For the SARC with photoluminescence, some of the absorbed solar energy is converted into the light emission, which reduced the thermal load from the Psun . Therefore, the effective absorbed solar energy (Psun ) is the absorbed energy (Pabsorption ) minus the emitted energy (Pemission ), as shown below:
(2)
P
sun
=
P
absorption
-
P
emission
=
( 1 - E S R ) P
solar
where effective solar reflectance (ESR) is derived from the photoluminescence QY, and the Psolar is solar radiation intensity. The above equations elucidate the intelligent characteristics of the radiative cooler induced by ESR. With the increase in outdoor solar irradiance, more solar radiation is absorbed by the RC. However, due to the presence of photoluminescent material with high quantum yield (QY), the amount of solar radiation transformed into fluorescence also increases. Consequently, the SARC exhibits superior cooling performance during daytime, and the temperature difference amplifies with increased solar radiation (Fig. 1 b).

To delineate the effective spectrum of the radiative cooler incorporating fluorescence, we recalculated its effective solar reflectance via simulation based on QY, photoluminescence absorption spectrum, and emission spectrum, as illustrated in Fig. 1 c. Further details are provided in Section 1, Supporting Information. Theoretically, when the quantum efficiency is.

100 %, all absorbed solar energy is converted to fluorescence, representing the maximum theoretical limit. The results demonstrate that a high quantum yield of SARC coating leads to an improved cooling performance. Therefore, compared with Normal RC without photoluminescence, the SARC exhibits adaptive reflectance. This implies that during periods of solar radiation, the reflectance of the SARC exceeds that observed during night-time. As shown in Fig. 1 d, we conducted a comparative analysis using representative works, evaluating both inherent static reflectance and adaptive reflectance [3–4,7,22,27,34–39]. The results demonstrate that our developed coating excels not only in reflectivity but also in adaptive performance, outperforming other coatings. Furthermore, we employ environmentally friendly polymer CDs as the photoluminescent material, compared to other metal-based photoluminescent materials. We then pioneered the development of Smart Cooling Beads (SCBs) as the universal matrix for carbon dots and demonstrated them as an adaptive urban skin with the scalable aqueous roof PDRC coating, as depicted in Fig. 1 e.

To produce the scalable SARC roof coating with photoluminescence, the SCBs were first fabricated by coating commercial hollow glass beads with fluorescent polymer nanoparticles containing carbon dots (FPs@CDs). Subsequently, this SCBs were combined with the SARC roof coating, as depicted in Fig. 1 e. The patented fluorescent polymer nanoparticles, FPs@CDs, consisted of three main components. Firstly, there was a hydrophobic core composed of poly(styrene-co-butyl acrylate) (Poly(St-co-BA)). Secondly, a hydrophilic polyethyleneimine (PEI) shell which is covalently linked to the core, forming a cohesive unit, as shown in Fig. 2
a. Lastly, carbon dots were embedded within the hydrophilic PEI shell by crosslinking with carboxylic acid, and they were evenly distributed throughout the shell. The morphology of the CDs-embedded polymer particles was illustrated in Fig. 2 b, revealing spherical shapes with high uniformity and an average diameter of 120 nm. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the particles displayed an even distribution of carbon dots on the particle shells (Fig. 2 c). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed, indicating an average hydrodynamic diameter of 167 nm in aqueous solution with a narrow size distribution (Fig. 2 d). The particles exhibited positive surface charges, with a zeta-potential of approximately + 42.6 (±0.8) mV. This high surface charge was attributed to the presence of water-soluble polyethyleneimine, which contained numerous protonable amine groups, thus resulting in excellent water dispersibility. To demonstrate the effect of carbon dots (CDs) on the fluorescent properties of these FP particles, we compared the fluorescence spectra of the two particles, as shown in Fig. 2e and Fig. 2f. We found that the fluorescent property of the CD-embedded polymer particles is strongly enhanced. As shown in Fig. 2f, strong emission is observed at 440 nm when excited with wavelengths ranging from 320 to 400 nm, indicating an excitation-independent characteristic. Moreover, the highest fluorescent intensity is observed at 440 nm when excited with 360 nm light. When excitation wavelengths are higher than 400 nm, the CD embedded particles show an excitation-dependent characteristic with much lower emission intensities. This illustrates a typical fluorescence emission process: upon excitation by a high-energy photon, nonradiative relaxation takes place, followed by radiative relaxation, resulting in the emission of a lower-energy photon.

To introduce fluorescent properties into more common materials and scale them up, we proposed Smart Cooling Beads (SCBs) by combining the previously prepared FPs@CDs with hollow glass beads. These SCBs were then used as photoluminescent fillers to make SARC coatings using an aqueous solution method. As shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b, the dispersion of the particles was coated onto the hollow glass beads through a process of direct mixing and drying. It was crucial to determine the appropriate weight ratio to achieve complete coverage of the microspheres. SEM images (Fig. 3b) suggest that a weight ratio of hollow glass beads to fluorescent polymer particles of 1:0.2 (w/w) can achieve the desired complete coverage of the microspheres. The surface of the SCBs is covered with these FPs@CDs, which are highly compatible with P(St-co-BA) latex due to their similar core chemical composition. Additionally, this simple method allows carbon dots to be introduced into hollow glass beads of different sizes, endowing them with photoluminescent properties and demonstrating their scalability, as shown in Fig. 3c. Therefore, we combined SCBs with an aqueous emulsion to prepare the SARC roof coating (Fig. 3d and 3e). The detailed material preparation and synthesis process can be found in Section 2 of the Supporting Information. In the composition of the SARC coating, hollow glass beads account for 28 % by mass, and styrene-acrylic emulsion constitutes 14 %, both of which are commercial materials. The mass fraction of the substance that contributes to photoluminescence, SCBs, is less than 2 %. Moreover, the production process uses environmentally friendly and aqueous solution-based methods, and this aqueous solution-based coating can be easily applied to building surfaces using a rolling technique.

Remarkably, the photoluminescence comparison experiment in Fig. 3 d revealed that the incorporation of SCBs made the SARC roof coating fluorescent. To quantify the energy conversion efficiency facilitated by the FPs@CDs, quantum yield (QY) measurements were conducted on the samples (Fig. 4
a). The hollow glass beads with multiscale cavity structures and a specific size range can enhance solar reflectance and infrared emittance in PDRC [39–41]. Upon combining FPs@CDs with hollow glass beads to form SCBs, the QY increased from 28 % to 31.8 %. Subsequently, when the SCBs incorporated into the PDRC roof coating, the QY exhibited a notable improvement, reaching 33.6 %. This enhancement may be attributed to the interaction between FPs@CDs and the Si-O bonds of the hollow glass beads through hydrogen bonding (H-Bond) within the fabricated PDRC coating, significantly influencing their electronic state. The successful integration of SCBs led to the PDRC roof coating demonstrating an enhanced photoluminescent QY of 33.6 %, emphasizing the potential for modifying the spectral properties of polymer CDs through surface modification.

In order to demonstrate the enhanced cooling capacity of the SCBs, we reconstructed the experimental spectra based on its QYs and photoluminescence spectrum [27]. The SARC coating has the photoluminescent properties under the solar radiation, compared to other static coatings. The details can be found in Section S1, Supporting Information. Fig. 4 b shows that the FPs@CDs has the highest QY at a wavelength of 360 nm. The QY of the fluorescent coating varies at different wavelengths, meaning that the ESR of a radiative cooler with fluorescent material differs from the actual measured solar reflectance. This is because some of the absorbed photons in certain wavelength bands are re-emitted in other wavelength bands. To simulate the effective spectrum of the radiative cooler with fluorescence, the experimentally measured spectrum needs to be recalculated based on the QYs, absorption spectrum of photoluminescence, and emission spectrum. Fig. 4 c demonstrates this, where the solid line represents the solar absorption measured by instruments, showing its ability to absorb solar energy. However, due to fluorescence, some of the absorbed solar energy is converted into light emission. This is shown by the dotted line, which represents the effective solar absorption of the SARC. The results indicate that the ESR of the SARC coating will be higher than the measured one by the UV–VIS-NIR spectrograph.

To quantify the contribution of fluorescence, the Effective Solar Reflectance (ESR) of the SARC roof coating is measured through calorimetric calculations [42]. The measured solar reflectance using a UV–VIS-NIR spectrometer cannot represent the SARC coating's cooling performance under sunlight, because the emission of SARC coating cannot be measured by the UV–VIS-NIR spectrometer. Details of these experiments can be found in Section S3 of the Supporting Information. Fig. 4 d shows the temperature rise curves of the SARC coating and a normal PDRC coating under indoor solar simulator conditions. The red line represents the SARC coating, while the blue line represents the normal PDRC coating with the same solar reflectance but no fluorescence. The results clearly demonstrate that the temperature of the SARC coating is significantly lower than that of the normal PDRC coating. This is because the fluorescent molecules inside the SARC coating convert absorbed heat energy into light energy, reducing the overall heat energy in the system. The blue area in the graph represents the enhanced cooling performance. The ESR of the test specimen can be determined by employing temperature interpolation, as shown in Fig. 4 e, which is approximately 95 %. This indicates that the contribution of fluorescence is around 2.5 %. Additionally, a comparison test was conducted under different gradients of solar radiation by controlling the solar simulator as shown in Fig. 4 f. As direct solar radiation increases from 220 W. m−2 to 880 W. m−2, the temperature difference between the SARC coating and the normal coating also increases from 1.5 K to 3.2 K. The results revealed that at higher solar radiation intensities, the temperature drop in the SARC coating was greater compared to the normal RC. This demonstrates the self-adaptive properties of the SARC coating.

To investigate the excellent cooling performance of the SARC coating, we conducted continuous outdoor measurements on both normal RC and SARC coating in Hong Kong. These measurements were performed on clear days with high humidity, as shown in Figure S2. The green line refered to the normal RC (Rnormal = 0.935), whereasthe other line refered to the SARC coating (Rsmart = 0.93), as depicted in Fig. 5
a. The details can be found in the Section 4 of Supporting Information. Both samples were placed side by side in foam insulated boxes, which served as experimental devices. We intentionally did not use PE as an insulating cover in order to simulate the scenario of the roof coating, above-ambient cooling. All the tests were conducted simultaneously under identical conditions, including air temperature and solar irradiance. Fig. 5 b demonstrates that both samples achieved a temperature drop of approximately 20 °C under sunlight, when compared to a real concrete roof. This result indicates that the introduction of SCBs in the SARC roof coating still maintains excellent radiative cooling performance. Furthermore, during daytime hours (from 8:30 a.m. to 5:30p.m.) under the sunlight, the temperature of the SARC remained lower than that of the normal RC coating (ΔT ∼ 0.5 °C), though the solar absorption of SARC is relatively higher than that of normal coating (difference of ∼ 0.5 %). Moreover, both samples exhibited similar temperatures during night time. This inconsistency in temperature difference during different times of the day suggests that the introduction of SCBs enhances the cooling capacity of the roof PDRC coating. In addition, the cooling performance of the SARC coatings did not show any degradation over three days, indicating the stability of the carbon dots under strong outdoor UV radiation, in contrast to fluorescent conjugated polymers. We also tested the SARC coatings after 27-day outdoor exposure (25 Sep. 2023 – 23 Oct. 2023), and found that reflectance in the solar spectrum only decreased by 1.7 %. Regarding maintenance measures, since we developed solution-based coatings, the surface spectral properties of SARC coatings can be easily maintained by re-coating them usingroller coating methods.

It's crucial to recognize that the cooling efficiency of materials isn't solely determined by their spectral properties; external climate patterns also significantly influence their overall effectiveness. Therefore, an assessment in various climate zones was conducted, mapping the average annual temperature drop and cooling power across major cities in China. This analysis was based on comprehensive weather data that included solar radiation and air temperature [43,44]. The details can be found in the Section 5 of Supporting information. Fig. 5 c depicts that in regions with high solar radiation, the higher temperature drop was achieved by SARC coating, ranging from 3.3 °C to 7.6 °C. This indicated that SARC coating is more effective in lowering temperatures in areas with higher solar radiation. Furthermore, Fig. 5e and 5f present statistical analysis of temperature data for Beijing throughout the year. They compare the average temperature drop achieved by SARC coating, compared to a.

regular roof and a normal PDRC respectively. The results demonstrate that the stronger the solar radiation, the greater the temperature difference achieved by SARC coating, which consequently lowers the energy consumption of the building. In addition, a resource map of the maximum net cooling power (Pnet) was created (Fig. 5 d), revealing that Pnet is higher in areas with higher temperatures and stronger solar radiation. This suggests that SARC coating is more beneficial in regions with these climatic characteristics. Furthermore, the cooling energy saving of three different roof coatings (commercial white coating, normal RC, and SARC coating) was compared across the 11 cities of China (Fig. 5 g). The results indicate that the SARC coating outperforms the other two coatings in terms of energy-saving performance, with potential energy savings of up to 500 MJ.m−2. This highlights the importance of regulating roof coatings and implementing solar modulation in building to achieve significant energy savings.

Here, we have successfully developed developed a scalable SARC roof coating by utilizating polymer CDs and investigate its adaptive cooling performance in buildings. Notably, compared to other metal-based photoluminescent materials, polymer CDs exhibit good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and easy surface functionalization. Therefore, the easy modification of polymers allows for the successful design of SCBs. Such a novel design of SCB marks the first instance of introducing polymer carbon dots into a SARC coating, offering a universal enhancement applicable to various matrix materials. The experimental data demonstrate that the influence of fluorescence contribution, quantified at 2.5 % for adaptive solar reflectance. Compared to normal RC coatings, especially those utilizing hierarchical structures with ultra-high reflectivity (>95 %), our SARC coating does not aim to achieve ultra-high reflectivity. Instead, our method is more effective in enhancing the cooling effect of materials with reflectance below 95 %, making it more suitable for building roofs and walls due to its unique photoluminescence properties. In future work, the synthesis of various carbon dots (CDs) to enable spectral modulation, and consequently, the preparation of SARC coatings in different colors using our developed method, will be necessary. This initiative aims to further advance the use of polymer carbon dots in SARC technology, paving the way for the subsequent development of smart urban skins.

Quan Gong: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ho Chun Wong: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration, Investigation, Data curation. Jianheng Chen: Software, Resources, Methodology. Pei Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Lin Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Q. Gong, and H.C.Wong, contributed equally to this work. The work described in this paper was substantially supported by the CRF (Project No. C5051-22GF), the NSFC/RGC Joint Research Scheme sponsored by the Research Grants Council of Hong Kong, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. N_PolyU513/18), The Hong Kong Polytechnic University through Projects of RISE (Project No. Q-CDAJ), and Research Institute for Sustainable Urban Development (Project No. 1-BBWX).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.153262.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Radiative human body cooling by nanoporous polyethylene textile

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hsu P-C, Song AY, Catrysse PB, et al. Radiative human body cooling by nanoporous polyethylene textile. Science. 2016; 353:1019-23.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Potential energy and climate benefits of super-cool materials as a rooftop strategy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Baniassadi A, Sailor DJ, Ban-Weiss GA. Potential energy and climate benefits of super-cool materials as a rooftop strategy. Urban Climate. 2019; 29:100495.
Li T, Zhai Y, He S, et al. A radiative cooling structural material. Science. 2019; 364:760-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Passive radiative cooling below ambient air temperature under direct sunlight

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Raman AP, Anoma MA, Zhu L, Rephaeli E, Fan S. Passive radiative cooling below ambient air temperature under direct sunlight. Nature. 2014; 515:540-4.
